name: files
context: Create, read, move, find, and manage files and directories with shell commands and permissions
info: File operations are the most common shell task for agents. This skill covers the complete file and directory lifecycle: creating files and directories (touch, mkdir), reading content (cat, head, tail, less), copying and moving (cp, mv), removing (rm, rmdir), searching (find, locate), inspecting metadata (ls, stat, file, du, df), managing permissions (chmod, chown, chgrp), working with symbolic and hard links (ln, readlink, realpath), and handling temporary files (mktemp). Every agent workflow involves file I/O — reading configuration, writing output, organizing project structures. See [[shell/bash]] for command execution, [[shell/pipes]] for combining file commands with pipes, and [[shell/processes]] for commands that operate on open file handles.
body:
# File and Directory Operations

Files and directories are the foundation of agent work. Every codebase, configuration, log, and artifact lives on a filesystem.

## Creating Files and Directories

```bash
# Create an empty file (or update timestamp)
touch newfile.txt

# Create file with content
echo "hello" > newfile.txt
printf '%s\n' "line 1" "line 2" > newfile.txt

# Create with heredoc (multi-line content)
cat > config.json <<'EOF'
{
  "port": 8080,
  "debug": false
}
EOF

# Create directory (and parents)
mkdir -p src/components/ui

# Create temporary file (auto-generates unique name)
tmp=$(mktemp)             # /tmp/tmp.XXXXXXXXXX
tmpdir=$(mktemp -d)       # /tmp/tmp.XXXXXXXXXX (directory)
```

## Reading Files

```bash
# Entire file
cat file.txt

# First/last N lines
head -n 20 file.txt
tail -n 20 file.txt

# Follow file updates in real time (logs)
tail -f /var/log/app.log

# Specific line range (lines 10-20)
sed -n '10,20p' file.txt

# Count lines, words, bytes
wc -l file.txt            # Lines
wc -w file.txt            # Words
wc -c file.txt            # Bytes
```

## Copying, Moving, Removing

```bash
# Copy file
cp source.txt dest.txt
cp -r src_dir/ dest_dir/      # Recursive copy
cp -a src_dir/ dest_dir/      # Archive (preserves permissions, timestamps, symlinks)

# Move / rename
mv old_name.txt new_name.txt
mv file.txt /other/directory/
mv *.log archive/              # Move multiple files

# Remove files
rm file.txt
rm -f file.txt                # Force (no error if missing)
rm -r directory/               # Recursive
rm -rf build/                  # Recursive + force

# Remove empty directory
rmdir empty_dir/
```

## Finding Files

```bash
# Find by name
find . -name '*.py' -type f
find . -iname '*.JPG' -type f         # Case-insensitive

# Find by modification time
find . -mtime -1 -type f              # Modified in last 24 hours
find . -mmin -30 -type f              # Modified in last 30 minutes
find . -newer reference.txt -type f   # Newer than reference file

# Find by size
find . -size +10M -type f             # Larger than 10MB
find . -size 0 -type f                # Empty files

# Find and execute
find . -name '*.tmp' -delete                          # Delete matches
find . -name '*.js' -exec grep -l 'TODO' {} \;        # Search in matches
find . -name '*.log' -exec gzip {} +                   # Compress matches

# Exclude directories
find . -path ./node_modules -prune -o -name '*.ts' -print
```

## File Metadata and Inspection

```bash
# Detailed listing
ls -la                     # Long format, all files including hidden
ls -lah                    # Human-readable sizes
ls -lt                     # Sort by modification time
ls -lS                     # Sort by size

# File details
stat file.txt              # Full metadata (size, permissions, timestamps)
file document.pdf          # Detect file type from content
du -sh directory/          # Directory size, human-readable
du -sh */ | sort -rh       # Subdirectory sizes, largest first
df -h                      # Filesystem disk usage
```

## Permissions

```bash
# chmod — change mode
chmod 755 script.sh        # rwxr-xr-x (owner:rwx, group:rx, other:rx)
chmod +x script.sh         # Add execute for all
chmod u+w,g-w file.txt     # Owner: add write; Group: remove write
chmod -R 644 docs/         # Recursive

# Common permission patterns
# 755 — executable scripts, directories
# 644 — regular files (owner writes, others read)
# 600 — private files (owner only)
# 700 — private directories/executables

# chown — change owner
chown user:group file.txt
chown -R user:group dir/

# chgrp — change group
chgrp developers file.txt
```

## Links

```bash
# Symbolic link (soft link) — pointer to a path
ln -s /real/path /link/path
ln -sf /real/path /link/path    # Force overwrite existing link

# Hard link — additional directory entry for same inode
ln original.txt hardlink.txt

# Resolve symlinks
readlink -f /link/path          # Full canonical path
realpath relative/path          # Absolute path resolving symlinks and ..
```

## Disk and Space

```bash
# Filesystem usage
df -h                           # All mounted filesystems
df -h .                         # Current filesystem

# Directory sizes
du -sh *                        # Size of each item in current directory
du -sh --max-depth=1 .          # One level deep
du -h --max-depth=2 . | sort -rh | head -20   # Top 20 largest

# Find large files
find / -type f -size +100M 2>/dev/null | head -20
```

## Safety Patterns for Agents

```bash
# Always check before destructive operations
[[ -f "$file" ]] && rm "$file"
[[ -d "$dir" ]] && rm -rf "$dir"

# Use trash instead of rm when available
trash-put file.txt   # (trash-cli package)

# Dry run with find -delete (list first, then delete)
find . -name '*.tmp' -type f       # Review matches
find . -name '*.tmp' -type f -delete  # Then delete

# Backup before overwriting
cp important.conf important.conf.bak

# Atomic writes via temp file + mv
result=$(process_data)
echo "$result" > output.tmp && mv output.tmp output.txt
```

sections.title: Creating files and directories
sections.body: Use `touch` to create empty files or update timestamps. Use `mkdir -p` to create directories with parents. Use heredocs (`cat > file <<'EOF'`) for multi-line content. Use `mktemp` for temporary files — always pair with `trap 'rm -f $tmp' EXIT` for cleanup. Use `install -m 755 -d dir/` to create directories with specific permissions in one command.

sections.title: Finding files
sections.body: The `find` command searches directory trees. Common predicates: `-name` (glob), `-iname` (case-insensitive), `-type f` (files), `-type d` (directories), `-mtime -N` (modified in last N days), `-size +NM` (larger than N megabytes), `-perm` (by permission). Actions: `-print` (default), `-delete`, `-exec cmd {} \;` (per file), `-exec cmd {} +` (batched). Use `-prune` to skip directories like `node_modules`. For indexed searches, use `locate` (requires `updatedb`).

sections.title: File permissions and ownership
sections.body: UNIX permissions: read (4), write (2), execute (1). Three groups: owner, group, other. `chmod 755` = rwxr-xr-x. Symbolic mode: `chmod u+x` (owner +execute), `chmod g-w` (group -write), `chmod o=r` (other = read-only). `chown user:group file` changes ownership. Directories need execute permission to be traversed. Scripts need execute permission to run directly. The sticky bit (`chmod +t dir`) prevents deletion of files by non-owners. The setuid bit (`chmod u+s`) runs a program as its owner.

sections.title: Disk usage and filesystem info
sections.body: `df -h` shows mounted filesystem usage. `du -sh dir/` shows directory size. `du --max-depth=1` breaks down by subdirectory. `stat file` shows detailed metadata: size, blocks, inode, device, permissions, timestamps (access, modify, change). `file filename` detects content type via magic numbers. `lsblk` lists block devices. `/proc/mounts` or `mount` shows current mounts.
